claims.proposition: The declared RQ8 payload uses fewer bytes than raw binary32 payload for one 2048-value group.
claims.conclusion: RQ8 uses 2113 payload bytes instead of 8192, saving exactly 6079 bytes.
premises: There is one signed quant byte per value.
There is one numerator byte per 32 values.
There is one exponent byte per at most 64 blocks.
A binary32 value occupies four payload bytes.
claims.warrant: Direct integer arithmetic over the declared representation.
claims.countercheck: The comparison excludes object headers, alignment, temporary buffers, and executable code.
claims.uncertainty: None for the payload count when the representation premises hold.
claims.boundary: Does not establish accuracy, encoding cost, latency, throughput, or model quality.

claims.proposition: The generated mirror kernel preserves the retained reference outcomes inside the checked 2048-column domain.
claims.conclusion: The deterministic release fixtures produced bit-equal binary32 output in both fresh verifier profiles.
premises: The direct and ASDF mirror suites each passed 37 checks.
Twelve deterministic differential cases passed in fresh semantic and measured correctness profiles.
The strict candidate disabled the public fallback during differential verification.
claims.warrant: Deterministic differential testing against the retained semantic oracle.
claims.countercheck: Unsupported graph shapes and incomplete targets were separately required to fail closed.
claims.uncertainty: The finite fixtures do not enumerate every valid input bit pattern or SBCL build.
claims.boundary: Does not establish a general compiler, cross-runtime portability, or material speedup.

claims.proposition: This alpha is more efficient than Mesh TensorFlow as a whole.
claims.conclusion: Not established by the available evidence.
premises: The alpha implements bounded Lisp kernels and one symbolic graph slice.
It does not implement the supported Mesh TensorFlow surface end to end.
No representative cross-system workload was run for this release.
claims.warrant: A whole-system comparative claim requires both comparable implementations and a representative measured workload.
claims.countercheck: A payload-storage deduction and bounded kernel tests cannot be generalized to end-to-end system efficiency.
claims.uncertainty: The missing implementation and benchmark prevent a supported direction of comparison.
claims.boundary: Future evidence may change this classification; the alpha does not.
